businessModel.text:
General Electric is an industrial conglomerate focused on three core businesses following years of portfolio simplification:
  
    GE Aerospace: The crown jewel. Aircraft engines (LEAP, GE9X) and aviation systems for commercial and military aircraft. The LEAP engine (co-produced with Safran) powers the Airbus A320neo family and is in high-demand given the aircraft backlog. GE9X powers the Boeing 777X. This segment generates the highest margins and most durable growth in the portfolio.
    GE Vernova: Energy infrastructure including gas turbines, steam turbines, power conversion, and grid solutions. The business is a beneficiary of AI data-centre power demand and grid modernisation spending globally. GE Vernova is a separate publicly traded entity following its 2024 spinoff.
    GE HealthCare: Medical imaging equipment (MRI, CT, X-ray), ultrasound, and digital infrastructure for healthcare. Spun off in January 2023 as a separately traded company. GE retains a meaningful stake.
  
  The investment case centres on the GE Aerospace franchise — one of the few truly dominant industrial franchises globally, with a large installed base generating recurring aftermarket revenue from spare parts, maintenance, and upgrades.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull Case
      75 / 100
      Global air travel demand grows at 5–6 per cent annually, driving sustained LEAP and GE9X engine demand. The aftermarket revenue cycle matures into a multi-decade high-margin stream. GE Aerospace achieves 30–35 per cent operating margins. The shares re-rate to 40–45× earnings as the market fully prices in the secular aviation growth story. Total returns of 15–20 per cent annually from here.
      Weight: 30%
    
    
      Base Case
      55 / 100
      Aviation demand grows at 4–5 per cent annually, a strong but not exceptional environment. LEAP production stabilises at current rates; aftermarket growth is steady. GE Aerospace margins expand to 28–30 per cent. Boeing recovery improves engine ordering. The shares trade at 33–40× earnings, delivering high-single-digit to low-double-digit annual returns.
      Weight: 50%
    
    
      Bear Case
      30 / 100
      Global economic slowdown reduces air travel demand, causing airlines to defer fleet expansion. Boeing's recovery stalls and 777X delays worsen. New engine development ( hydrogen, SAF-compatible) requires significant R&D spend. GE Aerospace margins compress and the multiple de-rates to 25× as the market prices in a more cyclical profile for the aviation franchise.
      Weight: 20%

recommendation.text:
Conviction Score: 56/100 — REDUCE
  At 37.7× earnings and 11.5 per cent below the 52-week high, the shares are not cheap and do not offer sufficient margin of safety for a high-conviction Buy. The aviation franchise is genuinely exceptional, but the valuation reflects this. Existing holders can maintain positions; new buyers should wait for a more attractive entry point below $260.
  
    Buy Below$260
    HOLD Zone$260–$320
    Reduce Above$320
    Stop / Sell$240

entryExit.text:
GE — General Electric Company (GE) — Entry / Exit Framework

Reduce / Trim: Above $314.52 — the stock is trading near or above the 52-week high. Capital should be rotated elsewhere.
Tighten or exit on any bounce above $339.19 — the risk/reward is poor at current levels.
Stop-loss (for remaining exposure): $355.45 — a close above the 52-week high would confirm the bull case is breaking down.
Target: $190.1 — partial mean reversion toward the middle of the 52-week range.
